name: Marian Anderson
abstract: Marian Anderson (February 27, 1897 – April 8, 1993) was an American contralto. She performed a wide range of music, from opera to spirituals.

concerts.label: Concert by the Martin-Smith Music School

about_quotes.speaker: Harry Edison
about_quotes.evidence: That’s where you go to hear Marian Anderson, and hear La Traviata, and Leonard Bernstein.
about_quotes.doc_title: Harry Edison — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Cedar Walton
about_quotes.evidence: people like Lena Horne or Billy Eckstine, Marian Anderson, were hosted to a, shall we say, a, a reception at the uh, the Hill's residence... I'd go with my autograph book.
about_quotes.doc_title: Cedar Walton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: David N. Baker
about_quotes.evidence: They would sponsor – they’re the people who sponsored the people like Marian Anderson and Jessye Norman and all those people who aspired to be classical musicians.
about_quotes.doc_title: David N. Baker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jean Bach
about_quotes.evidence: he said, 'Marian Anderson.' But you see how smart he was? That would impress a little college girl more. 'Marian Anderson’s going to be in an opera.'
about_quotes.doc_title: Jean Bach interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, October 18, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Kenny Davern
about_quotes.evidence: I mean Marian Anderson when she sang “St. Louis Woman” with her “diamond ring” had about as much to do with blues as the man in the moon.
about_quotes.doc_title: Kenny Davern interviewed by Michael Woods, Clinton, New York, September 23, 1995 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Norman Brownlee
about_quotes.evidence: I think Madame Schumann-Heinck gave an audition, as it was, to Marian Anderson, a colored woman, very fine voice, very fine person.
